concepts.title: Kirchhoff's Laws
concepts.description: Kirchhoff's First Law states that the sum of currents entering a node is equal to the sum of currents leaving the node. Kirchhoff's Second Law states that the sum of potential differences in a loop is equal to zero.

concepts.title: Potentiometer Method
concepts.description: The potentiometer method is used to measure emf and internal resistance of a cell. It is a more accurate method than the Wheatstone bridge method.

concepts.title: Ohm's Law
concepts.description: Ohm's Law states that the current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across it and inversely proportional to its resistance (I = V/R).

concepts.title: Resistors in Parallel
concepts.description: To remember that resistors in parallel add up in the formula (1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ...), use the phrase 'PARALLEL RESISTORS ADD UP'.

concepts.title: Confusing Kirchhoff's Laws
concepts.description: Students often confuse Kirchhoff's First Law with the principle of conservation of charge, and Kirchhoff's Second Law with the principle of conservation of energy. Make sure to apply the laws correctly in different situations.

concepts.title: Units of Resistance
concepts.description: The unit of resistance is the ohm (Ω), which is defined as one volt per ampere (1 Ω = 1 V/A).

concepts.title: Wheatstone Bridge Method
concepts.description: The Wheatstone bridge method is used to measure unknown resistance by balancing the potential differences across two sets of resistors. It is a popular method in current electricity experiments.

concepts.title: Potential Difference and Electric Potential
concepts.description: Potential difference is the work done per unit charge in moving a charge from one point to another, while electric potential is the potential difference per unit charge.
